content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Hey, it's a party of my favorite people.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Good afternoon, folks.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Thank you. Well, I'm assuming I'm in that group. You might have been talking about Deb and Wesley, and I wouldn't have known, so...

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: I mean, you are and your favorite wet.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: You're my favorite Jeremy. Does that help? He's my favorite Jeremy. I... Jeremy used to be fair. So...

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: I've only heard of two other ones. So. I'll take what I can get. You know.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: You're my favorite New Englander, which my husband next to me is probably going to freak out that I even have a favorite. So we just went to the Yankees game yesterday, if that makes any sense of why.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I should've in ited it.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Yes, if he heard you say wicked, he would probably like to chuck something at the computer.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Though... [Laughter] It's looking funny. I didn't mean to say that just happened. [Laughter].

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Yeah, you do a lot. I love it.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text:
I didn't realize. I do it. I have one friend, he now lives in Dorchester, which is basically Boston, and whatever you think of when you think of, like, Boston accent and mannerisms, like, he that's just how he is. He's the, like, caricature of Boston.
It's. It's pretty funny.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Whatever I think of. So he's got to walk around with a trash can so that all of the trash that comes out of his mouth goes in there, right?

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: He doesn't have the trash can, but he does have the trash.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: So he's littering too? My goodness. Sorry, I married into a Yankee family so I have to hate Boston like it's required. I understand.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
I mean, that's a real... I used to live in New York, and that's almost indoctrinated in you as you go about your everyday life. Did you see the game last night? I'm like, "Yeah, but I don't care that much." You need to...
Here's why.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Wow, whoa, you do care, you do.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: See? Exactly.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
I honestly said to Jeremy, "I will be 100% honest. I have met since meeting my husband, and I have met so many people from Massachusetts and Boston."
He always says, "They will always show themselves too, or whatever."

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Yes.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
People that I think are the nicest, one of the people that was going to be a babysitter for us. We called their references, drug issues, like every time, there's always something.
So you're the only person that I have known for over a month that has not had something come up, so kudos to you.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Thank you. Glad I can be in that elite list of people.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: You prove you're proving him wrong. So that's kind of fun.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I hope to be that guy. Guys, I'm going to start the recording.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Go ahead.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Yeah, so start recording. All right. So just gathering a few folks to help with doing some initial pre-gaming of how we want to go about validating the system that we're about to deploy. A couple of ideas in different conversations. One excellent idea was this idea of... We already had a lot of work done and validation done. Devin and Jeremy, this is Jeremy's idea. We had a lot of validation done by virtue of the Shopify implementation.
So are there any scripts, playwrite scripts, or are there any other test scripts or even load testing? So anything like that we can leverage a playbook and anything that we can leverage to allow us to effectively walk the system.
I think to walk the system, we need to be clear and... There needs to be an actor perspective added to that. So walk the system from the perspective of a customer, walk the system from the perspective of an admin user, from the perspective of just doing a data check.
So want to start there? Another idea. I think Sam actually shared this idea in the earlier conversation. Data coming in ultimately can be actioned by the administrator, by the admin team, by the customer service team.
So in theory, if we were able to place an order, whatever that order looks like, we should be able to prove it's how it looks on the back and to see... By looking at the view from the customer perspective.
So a good idea. The third idea I'd like to just open the floor with is from Antonio. Antonio has been working on doing an end-to-end automated test using some recurrent APIs using some own custom code that allows us to take an order from creating a recurring commerce order all the way up to what does it look like in a projection, right?
Too might represent that correctly.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: You are... Yeah, that's pretty much what we are doing, except by the fact that we are using the native SDK to interact with recurrly. But it's pretty much the API.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
So I think that is valuable. I think one thing I'd post to the team is there an opportunity for us to evolve that to do verification? You going through the APIs that we expose in the Supergraph specific for the customer portal.
So that's the high level. Let me open the floor maybe. Let's start with Devin, Jennifer, Jeremy, walk us maybe through this, help us understand the experience of validating Shopify, and see if there's anything we can start pulling from that.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text:
I think when we first did it, we had a lot of QA testing looking at the order in C star because C star of the portal in Devon. You know, tell me if I'm wrong here. But really, it kind of gives you a lot of information about the order.
So you can once you're given like the order guit or whatever or you went into Shopify and didn't order, you're able to find it pretty easily and then kind of be able to check everything there. And then as far as FSA, I think that was something too, where you had to log into.
I've never used FS a so, you know, I don't really know I'm talking about but in FSA, you kind of can go in there or someone can and kind of look at it through the front and like I don't know if we had any automated tests.
I think it was a lot of QA Devon, does that sound right?

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text:
Yeah. We would create the orders in Shopify and sort of check the handshake on the data transferred to CTAR and then for FSA we can't really, process because it would take a day away from an actual team.
But we could open up. That screening and just sort of check that the. You know, a few of the things like the orders amount, paid participant information, things like that, without, you know, fully processing those tests ordered.
And I met with, Beth earlier this week, and I had a, sort of a checklist of validations that we could do, so I'll share those in the chat.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Would we want to start testing in the pre-prod environment rather than production so that we can do some more order validation without worrying about taking orders away?

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: Yeah, that's a good idea.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
I mean, I think we should run the scenarios around card verification and age verification, things like that. I think most of those are covered natively within Recurly, but it's still worth it. When you say you have a list, do you mean you literally have a test script that X users do? Or do you have just, like, a bulleted list?
Like, check this all, like, verify the customer fails on an invalid card? Like, is that what you have?

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: Right?

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Or step?

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: More so, the latter.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, I'd love to, actually. Can you? I'll send you a link in Confluence. I'd love to put it there and just let that be a point of collaboration for the team. Let me send you...
I'll send you a space underneath inside of the Confluence page, but keep going. Okay, well let me give a hint to the next step here. We have QA tasks or a checklist of QA activities that we can run through. I guess... Devin, I'd task you with reviewing that list, adding to that or subtracting, updating relative to recurring specifically.
For example, subscriptions might be slightly different, right? I would include the idea of points of verification. So in our system is one point in recurring, another point in C stars, another point, right? In theory, projections and customer service are other points, but I think those are the core ones because the others should inherit from them. Does that exist? What? I'm asking.

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: Yeah, I'm not sure. If I have access though to recurring.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I mean, we can facilitate.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: We can get you access.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, so that's the initial... That's the functional pass like user functional pass. I do very much like the work that Antonio did relative to automating some of this.
So I guess... Antonio, what are your thoughts on can we add in the valid...? If we were to think about it as the Supergraph currently has all the reads, all the queries that we would need to power the customer service experience, right?
So in theory, we should be able to trace back from creating an order. Seeing it in a projection. Seeing it inside of... Sorry. Seeing inside of the current... Seeing it inside of the customer service screen, which is just a view on top of CSTAR.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: Correct? Yeah, I do agree.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
How would we...? I guess my question would be, "Do you think that's a valuable activity for us to add?"
Then, is it a Herculean activity? Something that... Can it be accomplished by, let's say, Tuesday Monday of next week?

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: The C-start side... I cannot speak for the C-start side. I'm not following close enough. The side of the work, but all the rest of it... For sure, we can target Monday as it's doable.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text:
You could just... I think it's still in progress, but there's a Lambda that's supposed to send the data based on that hydration. A CL LLaMA or something to send the recurring order placed event to the event grid.
So as long as there is something set up in the non-fraud environment like that sends the data to the test event grid, you could hypothetically or potentially... Just link your workflow to actually hit the non-prod Lambda that sends data into the event grid. Then Devon could trigger whatever type of test he's doing from that workflow, and then he doesn't have to manually go in and do an order in Recurly or something.
So... Is that more what you're looking for? Something like that just an end-to-end pipeline or get-it action that will put data in each of the different spots, and then we can have people spot-check it or just make sure that's not breaking.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
I would discourage us from doing anything where we're adding in a non-production Lambda function. Ideally, in my mind, we place an order in sandbox. There's a dev instance for the event. There should be a dev instance for the event grid. Ideally, there's a dev instance for the Lambdas that are the event processors that we spoke about yesterday, so all those should have their own dev instance.
So once we place an order, it should be able to make it into a dev instance of Csar with no manual interaction on our part other than placing the order. I think that's the ideal test case. Then what we should be talking about is how I can look into that instance now.
So ideally, coming from the perspective of Csar, what is the data that we are accessing in Csar? I wouldn't inject anything that's touching the event grid at all. I try to have the system run independently.
Then we're looking for observability into Csar after the fact after Csar has already had everything.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: As automated... Or you're saying that's where dev comes in?

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I think the Devin's portion I think is pretty seems pretty standard and clear to me.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Because I don't...

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I think I'm challenging the conversation around "Can we automate anything relative to this?" And that's where Antonio's script or modification of Antonio's work might come in.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text:
I can say that cstar is a side effect from the web hook and the ACL LLaMA. So eventually, once we trigger, you know, we run these ant end testing, those orders will end up on sister in any way. If we don't have an dev environment for, C Star now we only can like, let blank the integration on the part.
Otherwise we will end up creating orders on the production environment. But. Yeah. The. The. The chain, the reaction chain will be propagated until. COR.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text:
Yeah, we have a test environment. So there's a test, there's technically a depth and a test in a prod, but we really just wire up our AWS non-prod environment into our Csar test environment. So we do have that available testing and AWS non-prod.
Like you said, obviously it's a side effect, but it's there.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Yeah, but...

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: That's correct. Okay.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Go AAD.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Great. That's perfect. Jeremy, as part of that test instance, do we have a test instance where for the customer portal for the customer service portal?

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Yes, there's a C-Star test instance, I'm looking at it right now.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: The API that powers or the communication layer that powers that user experience it. I assume that's not in our Mono. Requ. I'm assuming that will be in Azure. Azure.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Correct. Yeah, once it gets to the event grid. All the other stuff is in Azure or on-prem, but we have tests in production instances. So for all the APIs, for all the front-end... So in theory if I was saying the AWS non-prod, we just map it or send whatever to our C-Star or legacy, whatever you want to call it, test environment.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, so I guess counterproposal to scripting that because we don't have access to those APIs or we just don't have practical experience integrating with them. At least on the Thrive side. Counterproposal, we can just have the B team members doing the verification.
So we would effectively... Devon would own the Thrive side and then someone else would own... Obviously Devon could do that as well, but someone else or another team member would own the C-Star side. What we would need to do is just figure out...
Okay, here are the data elements I'm sending. Then they would have to have some kind of check-off and say, "Okay, here's what I'm getting. Here's how I understood the order." So we can make that a manual process rather than a coded process.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Yes, yeah, we can do that.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Okay, so then, are we...? Sound like we're stepping back from the idea of scripting the confirmation coming out of CSTAR.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text:
Yeah, I don't think there's any sort of. I like. Again. I'm not really familiar super familiar with the some of the legacy stuff, but I don't think there's any sort of thing like it's all like dont applications written in c sharp there's no playwright. I don't think there's really anything that can tell you.
Like, hey, here's an order and here's how it looks in every system. Maybe if we're lucky, there's some sort of storage procedure or something or scripts that the DBAS have that would be able to just tell us whether or not an order is like good, quote unquote.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
You what? For? What.
It was with can.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: But that's. That's something I kind of doubt. And we'd have to ask them if they have something like that. So, there's that.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Can I ask you to just... Going to ask you by virtue of the fact that you've been working on the ECOM API, which is doing much of this work, can you...? Can I ask you just to reach out to the DBAs and ask them explicitly that, "Do they have a mechanism? Is there an easy mechanism that we can use to help verify orders that are injected into CTAR?"

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Yeah, I don't know who to ask, but I can figure that out. Yeah, that's the only thing I think I'd say.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: DJ.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Okay. I'll ask DJ about it, but yeah, I mean, there's not really a scripted or automated thing that I know of, and I'll ask DJ about it, but that would give us some sort of health check on an order to make sure that anything inserted was done correctly.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: What I've historically done is...

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: I know. As far as FSA, that is required to be manual. Devon probably has the most experience checking an FSA for orders.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
So it sounds like the playbook is our best mechanism to actually prove out the system.
Then the team here would need to focus more on, "Are we covering all the test cases within that playbook?" What do we need to run the actual full playbook and verify everything inside of it?
From the three points, the order placed in the Recurly order and inside CCS, and then the current being the secondary data store there. Jennifer, any additional thoughts here? It sounds like the playbook is where the most effort should be, and it's going to be a manual effort for us to just really go through.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
Yeah, I think that's probably going to be the right thing. John did recently what's it called? Flush like he put in some events and everything there was... We were really lacking how many events and how many screenings were available in the prep production environment.
But that's actually gotten fixed just recently, so that's not going to be an issue. We should have events. But I would say start off with most of the testing being in preproduction and then once we've validated...
We feel like that's going really well. Then let's start doing production. That way we're not taking events away from people, just like Devon was saying.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Agree. Tony, do you know? Maybe Jennifer, you notice as well, do we know if all events are all possible events in the webhook? The eight that we know of are... Are all those firing inside of the sandbox only like the create order?

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text:
So, they should. But the end-to-end test cannot make some intermediate subscription webhook. Being fired, we should reach out to the Recurly team just to understand why it's not happening.
But they have those available and we are interacting via SDK in order to make that happen. But they are not happening. Happening so we could so far collect or how can I say be receive the or the paid one subscription created and deleted from those we selected. Those three are the ones that we are getting from them.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: What about renew?

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: There are way more webhooks available. There are way more subscription states available we could use. But yeah, it may be some sandbox limitation, but I must confess I haven't double-checked the information yet.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Do you know about renewal?

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text:
We do have a... They have a specific one for renewal, but that one has not been used on the test yet. We can just include that in our configuration and set up a test and start using that one as well.
But yeah, there is a specific webhook for subscription renewal.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, can I ask you just to test that? Because if that one doesn't work, then we don't have a way of proving our system without first going to production.
We need to get that list of things that we really only need the order placed and subscription renewed for this bridge phase.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: Yes.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
So those are the critical ones in my mind if we need to prove those work.
So if you could just double-confirm on the renewal if it will trigger inside of sandbox, and if it's not, just give us a quick write-up so we can send that over to Rick Earli. I would ping you, Elvis, because to his credit, he's gotten pretty deep in the documentation on Rick Earli, so he may have an insight there for us.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text:
Okay, I...
Actually, we... I'm sorry, I was double-checking while we're talking. Actually, we are expecting that one. I will just double-check the test scenario. But those I'm sharing are all the available options and the selected ones.
Yeah, I need to take a look at the to-do and test if we are provoking the subscription until we receive this renewed one. I'll keep you guys posted. Otherwise, we have to get in touch with the team.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
I guess the counter to that question or the natural follow-up to that question would be, "Of these other subscription ones, are there any that we need to make sure we can act on?" For example, the canceled. I guess this is mostly a question for you, Jennifer.
We don't have the mechanism currently to do anything with canceled other than storing it inside the DB.

content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text: Yeah, so far we are not. There is no side effects for those. Yeah, sorry.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Jennifer, we are not pulling any canceled stuff over into CTAR.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, right, stay the course. All right. Sounds like the next step here is really just button up two things. The confirmation on the sandbox specifically for renewal. That's urgent. Jeremy, you're going to reach out to the DBA team to see if there's anything that they can provide or suggest relative to confirming orders that come in to CTAR.
Devin looks like the majority of this is going to be on getting that playbook to be bulletproof. I think, Jennifer, you and I would probably just need to put together a rough plan of how we want to tackle going through and verifying the system. Just maybe even just high level this day we are taking care of this activity. Something to that effect. Obviously, folks will run however they see fit as well, but just so we're checking off everything, anyone have anything to add to that subtract from that disagree like not prescribing here, open conversation.
All right, I'll summarize this up. Devin, if I send you a link to Confluence as soon as I get back to my desk, we can... If you can just push the playbook there, I'll take a read through, and then we can start seeing if we can build it out a little bit more.

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: Okay, sounds good. I'll have to add the FSA portion, but other than that, it should be good.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Nice. Any lessons learned from like the from running this test during for Shopify like you mentioned FSA as an example, like anything like that or anything that you guys ran into that we weren't able to verify or kind of didn't accurately test the system like those. That lessons learned is probably the most important thing you guys can contribute at this point.
So you put a think on that. Feel free to throw it in the document. Just throw a Slack. Stop saying Slack. Throw a team's message back.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: We all wish it was Slack.

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: Don't... [Laughter] I know what you mean when you say Slack.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: So much better.

content.speaker: Speaker 5
content.text: So I mean, as long as we understand, you know, [Laughter].

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Yeah, very else.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: If we keep saying it, maybe teams will just turn into Slack.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: You think they just spent the time and cloned some of their features, man? Come on, there's no shame in copying.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: I don't know. If this tried to copy it, it'd probably look like... Not very good.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Design is another pain point as well. Not just features, but design as well. Absolutely. All right, I give everyone back a couple of minutes in there. Date. Thank you guys so much for the time.
Let's... We'll start collaborating around that document that Devin shares, and I'll take a pass, maybe throwing together a rough calendar. All right, guys, enjoy the rest of the afternoon. See you in a bit.

content.speaker: jeremy.campeau@llsa.com
content.text: Have a good one. Thanks. Thank you, everyone.
